From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

American film producer and director (born 1931)

| Irwin Winkler | |

**Irwin Winkler** (born May 25, 1931) is an American film producer and director. He is the producer or director of over 58 motion pictures, dating back to 1967's *[Double Trouble](/wiki/Double_Trouble_(1967_film) "Double Trouble (1967 film)")*, starring [Elvis Presley](/wiki/Elvis_Presley "Elvis Presley"). The fourth film he produced, *[They Shoot Horses, Don't They?](/wiki/They_Shoot_Horses,_Don%27t_They%3F_(film) "They Shoot Horses, Don't They? (film)")* (1969), starring [Jane Fonda](/wiki/Jane_Fonda "Jane Fonda"), was nominated for nine [Academy Awards](/wiki/Academy_Awards "Academy Awards"). He won an Oscar for [Best Picture](/wiki/Academy_Award_for_Best_Picture "Academy Award for Best Picture") for 1976's *[Rocky](/wiki/Rocky "Rocky")*. As a producer, he has been nominated for Best Picture for four films: *Rocky* (1976)*, [Raging Bull](/wiki/Raging_Bull "Raging Bull")* (1980), *[The Right Stuff](/wiki/The_Right_Stuff_(film) "The Right Stuff (film)")* (1983), and *[Goodfellas](/wiki/Goodfellas "Goodfellas")* (1990).

## Early life and education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Irwin_Winkler&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Early life and education")]

Winkler was born on May 25, 1931 to a [Jewish](/wiki/Jewish "Jewish") family[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2) in New York City, to Sol and Anna Winkler. Growing up in [Coney Island](/wiki/Coney_Island "Coney Island"), one of his first jobs was on a bumper ride on the boardwalk.[[3]](#cite_note-maron-3) Winkler graduated early from high school and got into [New York University](/wiki/New_York_University "New York University"), but felt out of place among the older and more mature students, many of whom were former [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") soldiers that had entered university under the [G.I. Bill](/wiki/G.I._Bill "G.I. Bill"). At the outbreak of the [Korean War](/wiki/Korean_War "Korean War"), he volunteered to join the [Army](/wiki/United_States_Army "United States Army") and was stationed in [Louisiana](/wiki/Louisiana "Louisiana") for two years.[[3]](#cite_note-maron-3) After completing his service, Winkler returned to New York University and went on to receive a degree in American Literature in 1955.[[4]](#cite_note-times-4)

## Early career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Irwin_Winkler&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Early career")]

Winkler's first job after graduating university was at the [William Morris talent agency](/wiki/William_Morris_Agency "William Morris Agency"). Among his first clients as an agent were comedians [Sammy Shore](/wiki/Sammy_Shore "Sammy Shore") and [Jackie Vernon](/wiki/Jackie_Vernon_(comedian) "Jackie Vernon (comedian)"), though Winkler says of himself he was a "mediocre" agent.[[5]](#cite_note-times2-5)

After meeting [Robert Chartoff](/wiki/Robert_Chartoff "Robert Chartoff"), who managed several comedians, including [Jackie Mason](/wiki/Jackie_Mason "Jackie Mason"), the two set up their own talent management company.[[6]](#cite_note-6) One of the "unsuccessful clients"[[3]](#cite_note-maron-3) the William Morris Agency allowed him to take with him at the time that he had options on, was the actress [Julie Christie](/wiki/Julie_Christie "Julie Christie"), whose screentest for *[Doctor Zhivago](/wiki/Doctor_Zhivago_(film) "Doctor Zhivago (film)")* they arranged.[[7]](#cite_note-wood-7) Through another of their clients, Winkler and Chartoff brokered the [John Schlesinger](/wiki/John_Schlesinger "John Schlesinger") film *[Darling](/wiki/Darling_(1965_film) "Darling (1965 film)")* to film producer [Joseph E. Levine](/wiki/Joseph_E._Levine "Joseph E. Levine"). This deal led them from talent management to film production.[[5]](#cite_note-times2-5)

## Career as film producer and director

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Irwin_Winkler&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Career as film producer and director")]

Forming the production company Chartoff-Winkler Productions, Winkler began producing films with his partner Robert Chartoff in the late 1960s. Their first effort (along with [Judd Bernard](/wiki/Judd_Bernard "Judd Bernard")), was [*Double Trouble*](/wiki/Double_Trouble_(1967_film) "Double Trouble (1967 film)") (1967) starring Elvis Presley . Their next film was [John Boorman](/wiki/John_Boorman "John Boorman")'s thriller *[Point Blank](/wiki/Point_Blank_(1967_film) "Point Blank (1967 film)")* (1967) starring Lee Marvin, which is now regarded as a classic. Adding [Sydney Pollack](/wiki/Sydney_Pollack "Sydney Pollack") to their production team for one project, they garnered critical acclaim for *[They Shoot Horses, Don't They?](/wiki/They_Shoot_Horses,_Don%27t_They%3F_(film) "They Shoot Horses, Don't They? (film)")* (1969). Their next film, *[The Strawberry Statement](/wiki/The_Strawberry_Statement_(film) "The Strawberry Statement (film)")* (1970), won the [Jury Prize](/wiki/Jury_Prize_(Cannes_Festival) "Jury Prize (Cannes Festival)") at [Cannes](/wiki/Cannes_Film_Festival "Cannes Film Festival"). Chartoff and Winkler achieved their greatest success yet with *[Rocky](/wiki/Rocky "Rocky")* (1976), which earned the Academy Award as [Best Picture](/wiki/Academy_Award_for_Best_Picture "Academy Award for Best Picture"). Subsequently, the producing duo picked up Best Picture Oscar nominations for *[Raging Bull](/wiki/Raging_Bull "Raging Bull")* (1980) and *[The Right Stuff](/wiki/The_Right_Stuff_(film) "The Right Stuff (film)")* (1983), their last project together before Chartoff-Winkler Productions dissolved in 1985.[[7]](#cite_note-wood-7)

Winkler produced such noteworthy features as *[The Right Stuff](/wiki/The_Right_Stuff_(film) "The Right Stuff (film)")* (1983) which received eight Academy Award nominations with four wins, *[Round Midnight](/wiki/Round_Midnight_(film) "Round Midnight (film)")* (1986) which garnered two Academy Award nominations. He then produced back-to-back Costa-Gavras films, *[Betrayed](/wiki/Betrayed_(1988_film) "Betrayed (1988 film)")* (1988) and *[Music Box](/wiki/Music_Box_(film) "Music Box (film)")* (1989), which was nominated and won the Golden Bear at the Berlin Film Festival, before receiving another Best Picture Oscar nomination for *[Goodfellas](/wiki/Goodfellas "Goodfellas")* (1990). He also produced *[Rocky II](/wiki/Rocky_II "Rocky II")* (1978), [Rocky III](/wiki/Rocky_III "Rocky III") (1982), *[Rocky IV](/wiki/Rocky_IV "Rocky IV")* (1985) and *[Rocky V](/wiki/Rocky_V "Rocky V")* (1990).

Winkler moved into the director's chair, debuting with *[Guilty by Suspicion](/wiki/Guilty_by_Suspicion "Guilty by Suspicion")* (1991), a drama (which he also scripted) about the Hollywood blacklist that starred [Robert De Niro](/wiki/Robert_De_Niro "Robert De Niro"), which was nominated for the Palme d'Or at the [1991 Cannes Film Festival](/wiki/1991_Cannes_Film_Festival "1991 Cannes Film Festival"). His second feature, a [remake](/wiki/Night_and_the_City_(1992_film) "Night and the City (1992 film)") of *[Night and the City](/wiki/Night_and_the_City "Night and the City")*, also starred De Niro and was chosen as the closing feature for the 1992 New York Film Festival. Winkler fared excellently at the box office as the writer-director of the [Sandra Bullock](/wiki/Sandra_Bullock "Sandra Bullock") vehicle *[The Net](/wiki/The_Net_(1995_film) "The Net (1995 film)")* (1995), which spawned a series of the same name debuting on the [USA Network](/wiki/USA_Network "USA Network") in 1998.

*[Life as a House](/wiki/Life_as_a_House "Life as a House")* (2001) made its world debut at the 2001 Toronto Film Festival[[8]](#cite_note-8) and told the tale of a depressed dying man ([Kevin Kline](/wiki/Kevin_Kline "Kevin Kline")) who funnels his energies into rebuilding the dilapidated beach shack he inherited from his abusive father and, in the process, building bridges between himself and his disaffected son ([Hayden Christensen](/wiki/Hayden_Christensen "Hayden Christensen")). After this film received critical praise, Winkler re-teamed with Kline for the follow-up *[De-Lovely](/wiki/De-Lovely "De-Lovely")* (2004), casting the actor as the lead in his biographical film about American composer [Cole Porter](/wiki/Cole_Porter "Cole Porter"), which centered on his unique relationship with his wife and [muse](/wiki/Muse "Muse") ([Ashley Judd](/wiki/Ashley_Judd "Ashley Judd")) and was chosen as the closing night film at the 2004 Cannes Film Festival.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

While his directorial career would last through 2006, Winkler continued to produce his share of films, including *[The Shipping News](/wiki/The_Shipping_News_(film) "The Shipping News (film)")* (2001), *[Enough](/wiki/Enough_(film) "Enough (film)")* (2002), the 2014 remake of *[The Gambler](/wiki/The_Gambler_(2014_film) "The Gambler (2014 film)")*—he'd also produced [James Toback](/wiki/James_Toback "James Toback")'s [1974 original](/wiki/The_Gambler_(1974_film) "The Gambler (1974 film)")—and his further return to the *Rocky* franchise with *[Rocky Balboa](/wiki/Rocky_Balboa_(film) "Rocky Balboa (film)")* (2006) and the [spin-offs](/wiki/Spin-off_(media) "Spin-off (media)") *[Creed](/wiki/Creed_(film) "Creed (film)")* (2015), which was nominated for multiple awards including 6 NAACP Image Awards, winning 4,[[10]](#cite_note-10) and *[Creed II](/wiki/Creed_II "Creed II")* (2018). His work with Scorsese continued on with *[The Wolf of Wall Street](/wiki/The_Wolf_of_Wall_Street_(2013_film) "The Wolf of Wall Street (2013 film)")* (2013), *[Silence](/wiki/Silence_(2016_film) "Silence (2016 film)")* (2016), and *[The Irishman](/wiki/The_Irishman "The Irishman")* (2019) which was Winkler's first collaboration with [Netflix](/wiki/Netflix "Netflix").[[3]](#cite_note-maron-3)

In 2019, Winkler published his autobiography, *A Life in Movies: Stories from 50 years in Hollywood*.[[11]](#cite_note-Wink-11) *[The New York Times](/wiki/The_New_York_Times "The New York Times")* wrote: "Winkler looks back fondly on a career producing some of the most successful films of the 20th century."[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Personal life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Irwin_Winkler&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Personal life")]

Winkler married his wife [Margo Winkler](/wiki/Margo_Winkler "Margo Winkler") in 1959 while still working at WMA in New York. They moved to Los Angeles in 1966. Margo was originally from California and her parents, Irma and Charlie Melson, were former [vaudeville](/wiki/Vaudeville "Vaudeville") performers.[[3]](#cite_note-maron-3) The couple have three sons, [Charles Winkler](/wiki/Charles_Winkler "Charles Winkler"), David Winkler, and [Adam Winkler](/wiki/Adam_Winkler "Adam Winkler"). Winkler's youngest son Adam is a professor of [constitutional law](/wiki/Constitutional_law "Constitutional law") at the [UCLA School of Law](/wiki/UCLA_School_of_Law "UCLA School of Law") and a published author.

### Honors

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Irwin_Winkler&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Honors")]

For his contribution to the motion picture industry, Winkler has a star on the [Hollywood Walk of Fame](/wiki/Hollywood_Walk_of_Fame "Hollywood Walk of Fame") at 6801 [Hollywood Boulevard](/wiki/Hollywood_Boulevard "Hollywood Boulevard"). He was also the recipient of the 2017 Producers Guild of America's [David O. Selznick](/wiki/David_O._Selznick "David O. Selznick") Achievement Award for his work in motion pictures and was awarded the Commandeur des Arts et Lettres by France.
